# str_pad

<center>
![](images/stringr_str_pad.png){style="width:200px"}
</center>

The function `stringr::str_pads()` adds white space to strings to ensure it is a minimum length.

[Tidyverse reference page](https://stringr.tidyverse.org/reference/str_pad.html)

## String vector

Prior to using the function create a vector with strings of length 1, 2, 4, and 8.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Create vector of strings
vec <- c("1", "to", "four", "_eight_")
#View vector
vec |> stringr::str_view()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] â”‚ 1
[2] â”‚ to
[3] â”‚ four
[4] â”‚ _eight_
```


:::
:::


## Default padding

Padding will add space to each string shorter than the specified pad length.
These strings will have a total length (i.e. number of characters) equal to the pad length.

By default spaces (` `) will be added to the start/left-side of the string for padding.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Pad strings to a min length of 10
vec |> stringr::str_pad(10) |> stringr::str_view()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] â”‚          1
[2] â”‚         to
[3] â”‚       four
[4] â”‚    _eight_
```


:::
:::


Strings with a length equal to or greater than the pad length will be untouched.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Pad strings to a min length of 4
vec |> stringr::str_pad(4) |> stringr::str_view()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] â”‚    1
[2] â”‚   to
[3] â”‚ four
[4] â”‚ _eight_
```


:::
:::


## Side

The side to add the padding characters can be specified.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Pad to the left, the default
vec |> stringr::str_pad(10, side = "left") |> stringr::str_view()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] â”‚          1
[2] â”‚         to
[3] â”‚       four
[4] â”‚    _eight_
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Pad to the right
vec |> stringr::str_pad(10, side = "right") |> stringr::str_view()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] â”‚ 1         
[2] â”‚ to        
[3] â”‚ four      
[4] â”‚ _eight_   
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Padd on both sides
vec |> stringr::str_pad(10, side = "both") |> stringr::str_view()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] â”‚     1     
[2] â”‚     to    
[3] â”‚    four   
[4] â”‚  _eight_  
```


:::
:::


## Pad character

The padding character can be specified.
This character can only have a length of one (i.e. "\t" or "||" cannot be used).


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Use pad character |
vec |> stringr::str_pad(10, pad = "|") |> stringr::str_view()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] â”‚ |||||||||1
[2] â”‚ ||||||||to
[3] â”‚ ||||||four
[4] â”‚ |||_eight_
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Use pad character .
vec |> stringr::str_pad(10, pad = ".") |> stringr::str_view()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] â”‚ .........1
[2] â”‚ ........to
[3] â”‚ ......four
[4] â”‚ ..._eight_
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Use pad character _ with padding on both sides
vec |> stringr::str_pad(10, pad = "_", side = "both") |> stringr::str_view()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] â”‚ ____1_____
[2] â”‚ ____to____
[3] â”‚ ___four___
[4] â”‚ __eight___
```


:::
:::
